sentences.text: So will be four tapes, right?
Just when we arrive in the concentration camp Krakau-Plaszow, we saw him screaming, yelling.
The dogs, lots of dogs, were barking.
And this is, just to see him was scary, you know, because we knew who he is.
People been talking.
People will been talking.
And they put us in barracks.
They been barracks with bunks, three-- one, two, three.
And together with my sister, we were in there.
Oh, yeah, separated.
And first, I worked with the brush factory.
And I saw this killing.
And then they moved me to the wash place when the laundry, I was washing the bloody sheets.
From Ghetto.
If they brought these up to Plaszow from Ghetto, because Ghetto was liquidated.
Was no more Ghetto.
They brought the bloody sheets to the laundry place.
And we were washing like this, by hands.
Oh, yeah, we knew.
Because they been shooting people in the Ghetto.
So and blood come very hard out.
So we have stoves there and big container with water and soap.
And we have to boil this.
And when I was not longer there, come a little Jewish man, and he asked for 10 girls.
This was '40-- 20.
I was 20 year old.
He hang my friend.
He wasn't my friend, he was much older.
But I knew the people.
He in very rich in Krakau there, a big store with leather, but very good people, very intelligent people.
But they knew he has lots of money, but not in concentration camp.
Already they took everything from him, and they hang him.
Yeah.
Not him, but he let him to hang.
They hanged him.
They have a place.
Yeah, we have to see this.
No, they didn't say.
They didn't need explanation.
They did what they want to do.
And poor man, he was a good, very religious man, good Jew.
And he left behind his wife and his daughter.
They been there.
Yeah.
Oh, he was going on the horse, riding horse.
Sometime we see him, but not very often.
It was very scary to see him, especially we hear barking the dogs.
This was so scary.
We didn't know what is going on.
And they are start to be the uppers.
Early in morning, they been blowing on the-- how you say?
On the whistles.
Everybody has to go out in winter, in summer, and stand in lines.
And they've been counting us, hours and hours.
Sometime was so terrible cold or so, so very hot.
They call upper, because if somebody didn't run away so they count.
Nonsense, you know, just to make hard the life, this what they did.
Yeah, everybody was scared.
He was a very scary person.
For example, he has a girl, she worked for him.
And he beat her daily.
And a guy, he worked for him.
Something was not clean enough what he wanted, he shoot him.
I think he was 17.
In his villa.
He has a villa, in the beginning.
How we work in the camp.
In the beginning, he has a villa.
Just that he was a monster.
This was not a human being.
And he make, Schindler, he make connection with him.
And he party with him.
And so was possibility that he let us go to Schindler.
Yes.
So he buy a factory.
I think this was one time a Jewish factory.
Schindler, Oskar Schindler.
And he owned the factory.
And he need people for work.
An armor factory.
Pots and pans we did there.
And--
Just for serving.
It was like business.
They sell to stores.
And one group was airport was men, so they came, work for Schindler.
And the girls who he select, the guy, we work for him.
The guy who took us.
He was a Jewish little guy.
And he came to the laundry place.
And he chose 10 girls.
I was among them.
No.
I don't know his name.
I cannot remember his face, only that he was, he was a little man.
And I was washing the laundry, and he say, come with me.
I didn't know first what is going to be, where I am going then.
Put us on a truck and took us, it's called Zablocie.
It was outskirt of Krakau.
There he has new factory and was a big sign, Emalja.
If you saw the movies, there the the emalja.
Yeah.
Is E-M-A-L-J-A.
Emalja, this means that the enamel, is in Polish emalja.
The enamel pots and pans was made there.
And was the owner was was Oskar Schindler.
Oskar Schindler.
So I worked in the factory, they put me there.
Right away, we work.
We live not so far from the factory.
We have to walk back and forth.
He built for us barracks.
Oskar Schindler, he built the barracks for us, for men and for woman.
Was much better than in Plaszow, all the condition was better.
The food was better.
The coffee was better.
And it was nicer, much nicer.
It was not three the banks, was only two.
We don't have to go so high and more comfortable.
These belong to Oskar Schindler, he builded this.
And this belong to him.
It was for all men.
And there I got this tattoo [INAUDIBLE] in the skin.
This is abbreviation of German word konzentrationslager.
Is like concentration camp.
You have this?
Konzentrationslager.
KL.
This means concentration camp.
The purpose was that we not run away.
That we are marked but--
No, this means that I belong to a camp as a prisoner.
And I am a prisoner.
If I run away, somebody see this, so they shoot me right away, the Germans.
Yes, but this was not that Schindler wanted, because he has his bosses also, you know-- SS and the German Nazi.
They did what they wanted.
So they say, you have here people is outside of the concentration camp, is a little concentration.
They can run away.
So they put this on us.
Everyone who was at Schindler has this.
All the Schindler's List Jews have--
Yeah, everyone.
Yes.
I work in his factory, so he make rounds every day.
And he make a great impression on me.
Here is the picture somewhere.
A tall, handsome man, always cigarette in his hands, lots of good perfume.
The whole factory was smelling if he come in, every day he came.
He has a big diamond on his finger that was shining, the diamond.
And always smile on his face, always a young girl on his side.
He never came by himself.
He wasn't very faithful to his wife.
But I am not criticize him, because he was good to us.
But what is the truth, I say.
And he make rounds and very, always smiling, always nice.
Only one time I speak with him.
And I ask him whether he can bring to his factory my father and my sister.
And he not promise me.
He say, it's not in his power to do this, so.
Yeah, he will talk to prisoners.
Everybody likes him, everybody.
But if came somebody like him, all the bosses, I told you, we make the sign six, that we are careful, that somebody is coming in factory and we have work.
Otherwise, if we didn't work a lot.
It's six, sign is six.

If they show this, nobody can know what this means, but we knew it, that it mean it come somebody to check-- whether it was Schindler, or whether was another German, or something, or somebody for checking us, they make six.
And then we stay by our work and start to work.
Otherwise, we didn't work a lot.
We weren't very productive in his factory.
We [INAUDIBLE] the pots and pans.
We put this in a solution where it was the enamel.
Then we put this on boards with nails, and carried this like that-- it was heavy-- to the oven.
And it bakes there.
And then was ready, the pots and pans.
It was in white and other colors.
Yes, please.
Just talking, and walking, and eating, and just trying not to work too hard.
Oh, yeah.
Not that we been starving, starving to death, but was not enough.
In this age, you need food [INAUDIBLE].
And even in his camp was better than in Plaszow or everywhere, but there was not so much food.
Was not enough.
We've been talking.
And was so many people and everybody have different talents.
For example, my one friend, she was a poet.
She writes poems.
And she was reading to us.
And she was very intelligent, very skilled girls.
And one was--
Frida, Frida.
Last name, I don't remember.
A long time ago, I know it, but I forgot.
Yeah, yeah.
One, she was very talented for making beautiful gloves from leather.
And they find out this, so they brought her leather and she make for the wife of Germans, she make the gloves.
And everybody did something.
But we, every day, we went to the factory to work.
How long I work?
Until the German, the Russian progress, and he is to liquidate the factory and move to Czechoslovakia.
And he moved us with him.
Oskar Schindler.
I know it was summer, and spring, and summer.
And then in November, then he move, they moved us.
Nobody, no at Oskar Schindler.
Never.
No in Krakow.
No in Brunnlitz.
Nobody was beaten.
Who was his people.
And not only this, he say to the German, you will not touch them.
They are my people.
He has guts, I tell you.
He spent money on us.
He was good to us.
He was a great man.
We know.
We know.
To bribe the Germans so they let us go.
We when for example, we been in Auschwitz.
Because he has to liquidate.
The Russian progress, so he has to liquidate.
And somebody say it was by mistake what I think.
We have to stop in Auschwitz, whole transport.
Well, we knew the Russian coming.
I from outside it cames.
From mouth to mouth, it came the news that the Russian progress.
So we knew.
And then they move us.
So we know.
They put us on train and we went.
It was a terrible feeling to come to Auschwitz, the chimneys, the smell of it.
We knew, we knew.
We knew in Ghetto.
Oh, yeah, we knew.
It was a terrible smell, hair and bodies.
You could smell.
And was very scary.
And we didn't know how long we were in there, whether one time we will go out.
They put us in shower.
And we didn't know what a shower is, just because we heard that gas they put in a shower.
But we really been the protection there from Schindler.
And he not let us go to the gas.
They cut our hair not completely, just short, very short.
He was behind us.
He said, this is my group.
They mark my people.
They good workers.
So probably, this where he need the money to bribe them.
Just we heard, we heard.
And we have a good feeling that he is with us.
They didn't treat us bad.
For example, they did not shave the hair, just leave short.
It will make a difference for girls.
And but I remember one time when, was when I was working at the laundry place, he came and he started to talk to us.
And he say a very bad time is coming.
I don't know what we will do.
Morlock. ?]
Before we went to Auschwitz.
This was in the wash machine, in the wash place, laundry place in Plaszow.
And he said the big [?
Morlock ?] is coming.
This what he says.
I still remember.
Yeah, this was still in the concentration camp on the cemetery.
No, we been working.
We been working there.
And when we belong to him, then he came to us, to the group.
And he say, the big [?
Morlock ?] is coming.
Morlock, ?] like the killer.
And he was worried.
He was worried about us.
And this what I think, he did everything, spent his money, and bribe them, and save us.
Before.
Before.
Yeah, then we went to him.
But we stop in Auschwitz
And then he had to liquidate the factory.
Yes, to move the factory.
And then went to Czechoslovakia.
He bribed them, probably he bribed them.
We didn't know all the details, you know.
We didn't have newspaper.
We didn't have radio.
We didn't know nothing, just what you could hear from people.
One month.
Yeah, and it was terrible feeling.
My sister, I met there.
I heard that this Madrit group is there.
So I was asking.
Madrit, where she was working.
M-A-D-R-I-T, Madrit.
Like the in Spain, this capital.
Like in Spain, Madrid.
His name was this, where she was working.
But he didn't have this power like Oskar Schindler to save them, so they went to Auschwitz.
And from Auschwitz, they killed them.
Yeah, I saw my sister.
I saw my father in Auschwitz too.
Yes, I did.
Where the man been.
So I been asking, do you see [?
Lukower? ?] And they say, no, go there, go there.
And I found him.
And I found my sister too.
Nothing, just sitting.
[LAUGHS] We were happy to see each other, but we didn't know the future.
He was good looking.
He was not skinny, like starving or something.
And I say, oh, you look good.
I was happy how he looked.
Nothing.
This was not work to do there.
We didn't do nothing, just waiting.
Also nothing.
No work.
This is worse than to work, you know that?
The day is so long and so boring.
This was another punishment, not to work.
And the uppers were there also in morning, early in morning get up.
In Auschwitz also.
The upper, what I say, they wake us up 5:00.
And we have to go out, everybody.
And they start to count us.
What we could say, we didn't know what will be.
But she say, I have a good feeling that you at Schindler, she say.
So the people say that compared to this, what we are in Auschwitz is like a sanatorium.
She, this is what she says to me.
This was the last time.
This was the last time in Auschwitz.
Yeah, they, when the Russian progress, they have to liquidate.
They put them on the tracks, took to the Baltic ocean, and sunk them.
Yes.
I don't know how.
Probably, on a boat, I think so.
After the war.
After the war, I was asking and asking.
I went to the Jewish Community Center in Krakow every day.
I went there every day, ask about.
About my mother I knew.
But I didn't know about my sister and father.
So I find--
I told you that he has the carbuncle here and got the--
Carbuncle it's called.
It is growth but is yellow from puss.
It's infection.
And so they give him the shot.
I, just I heard.
I don't know the details.
That he got the infection.
And instead to heal this what was not a problem, it was not penicillin this time, but still they could help him.
Was not during the war.
And they give him a shot, a poison shot and he passed away.
This what I heard.
Yes.
Yes.
I just know that came the trucks.
They put us on the train.
It was November, was snowing.
And with the train, we went to Brinice, to this place.
Brinice it's called this place.
B-R-I-N-I-C-E.
This was in Czechoslovakia, Moravia part from Czechoslovakia.
There he established another factory.
But not the enamel factory, but arms.
Yeah, he worked on arms.
Yes, yeah.
Oh, it was completely different-- different machines and different work.
But what we heard that the arms was not good.
What he did didn't work.
They didn't work.
But he, they didn't blame him.
They didn't kill him for that.
But this what we heard.
